title: Screenplay Format Specification
description: A machine-readable specification of screenplay formatting rules across industry variants. Covers page setup, element formatting, pagination, title page, and regional/medium conventions. This schema defines the *format contract* — not screenplay content.
x-disclaimer: No information within should be taken for granted. Any statement or premise not backed by a real logical definition or verifiable reference may be invalid, erroneous, or a hallucination. Verify against primary sources (e.g., Writers Guild formatting guides, Final Draft documentation, BBC Writers Room guidelines).

x-references.description: De facto standard screenplay software, element definitions

format_id.description: Opaque unique identifier for this format specification instance.

schema_version.description: Semantic version of the schema this instance was authored against. Allows tooling to select the correct validator without requiring a const that breaks every future version bump.

elements.description: Ordered list of element format definitions. Each element type (scene heading, action, dialogue, etc.) has its own formatting rules. Order reflects typical visual hierarchy but does not constrain content sequencing.

scene_numbering.description: Scene numbering conventions. Typically absent in spec scripts, present in shooting scripts.

revision_tracking.description: Color-coded revision page conventions used in production drafts.

notes.description: Free-text notes about this format variant, e.g. deviations from the base standard or studio-specific overrides.

extensions.description: Studio- or tool-specific extension properties. Use namespaced keys (e.g. 'finaldraft.template_id') to avoid collisions.

FormatVariant.description: Identifies which screenplay format tradition this spec belongs to.

variant_type.description: Discriminator for the format tradition. 'hollywood_spec' is the global default for unsolicited feature submissions. 'hollywood_shooting' adds scene numbers and revision colors. 'tv_multi_camera' uses distinct spacing and capitalization rules. 'french_continuite_dialoguee' is a prose-oriented format. 'custom' requires a note explaining the deviation.

label.description: Human-readable label for this variant, e.g. 'Hollywood Spec Script (Feature Film)'.

region.description: ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code for the primary region of this format tradition.

medium.description: The production medium this format targets.

language.description: BCP 47 language tag for the primary language of scripts using this format.

page_time_ratio.description: Approximate screen time per page. The Hollywood standard targets ~60 seconds/page in Courier 12pt. Multi-camera TV is roughly 30 seconds/page due to double-spacing.

PageSetup.description: Physical page dimensions, margins, and base font. These define the canvas on which all element formatting is applied.

line_spacing.description: Default line spacing for the body. Individual elements may override this.

method.description: Physical binding method. Hollywood standard uses two or three brass brads through three-hole punched pages.

brad_positions.description: Vertical positions of brads from page top.

format.description: Named paper format. Hollywood standard is US Letter. A4 is common in European and Asian industries.

Margins.description: Page margins. The left margin is typically wider to accommodate binding.

left.description: Left margin. Typically 1.5 inches in Hollywood format to allow for three-hole binding.

right.description: Right margin. Typically 1.0 inches. Some elements (dialogue, parenthetical) have their own narrower effective margins.

Measurement.description: A physical measurement with explicit unit declaration.

unit.description: Unit of measurement. Hollywood convention uses inches; European variants may use centimeters or millimeters.

Font.description: Typeface specification. Courier 12pt is the industry standard because it is a monospaced font that produces the page-per-minute timing approximation.

family.description: Font family name. 'Courier', 'Courier New', 'Courier Prime', and 'Courier Final Draft' are the accepted industry fonts.

size_pt.description: Font size in typographic points. The industry standard is 12pt.

monospaced.description: Whether the font is monospaced. MUST be true for screenplay formatting; the page-per-minute rule depends on fixed character width.

characters_per_inch.description: Horizontal character density. Courier 12pt yields 10 characters per inch.

lines_per_page.description: Approximate lines of text per page at single spacing with standard margins. Courier 12pt on US Letter with standard margins yields approximately 55-56 lines.

type.description: Spacing type. Hollywood single-camera uses single spacing for action, single for dialogue. Multi-camera TV uses double spacing for dialogue.

exact_value_pt.description: Exact line spacing in points. Only used when type is 'exact'.

ElementFormat.description: Formatting rules for a single screenplay element type. Each element (scene heading, action, dialogue, etc.) has distinct indentation, capitalization, spacing, and alignment rules.

aliases.description: Alternative names for this element type, e.g. ['slug line', 'master scene heading'] for scene_heading.

spacing_before.description: Vertical space inserted BEFORE this element.

spacing_after.description: Vertical space inserted AFTER this element.

alignment.description: Horizontal text alignment within the element's effective margins.

line_spacing_override.description: If present, overrides the page-level line spacing for this element only.

max_width.description: Maximum horizontal width of the text block for this element. Dialogue, for example, is narrower than action.

syntax.description: Content syntax rules — prefixes, suffixes, permitted patterns.

continuation_rules.description: How this element handles page breaks: (CONT'D), (MORE), etc.

items.description: Typographic modifiers applied to this element.

style_modifiers.description: Default typographic styling. Scene headings are sometimes underlined in shooting scripts.

page_break_behavior.description: How the element interacts with page boundaries. Character names should keep_with_next to avoid separation from dialogue.

extensions.description: Per-element extension properties for software-specific formatting overrides.

ElementType.description: Discriminator for the type of screenplay element. Core elements (scene_heading through transition) are present in all variants. Extended elements (shot, dual_dialogue, montage_header, etc.) are variant-dependent. TV-specific elements (act_break, cold_open_header, end_of_act, teaser_header) appear only in television formats.

Indentation.description: Horizontal positioning of the text block relative to the page's left margin.

left_offset.description: Distance from the left margin to the start of this element's text.

right_offset.description: Distance from the right margin that this element's text stops at (effective right boundary).

Capitalization.description: Text case rules for this element.

rule.description: The capitalization rule. Scene headings and character names are ALL CAPS in Hollywood format. Action is sentence case. 'as_written' means the author controls case.

exceptions.description: Documented exceptions to the capitalization rule, e.g. 'First appearance of a character name in action lines is ALL CAPS regardless of action line's sentence_case rule.'

Spacing.description: Vertical spacing measured in blank lines.

lines.description: Number of blank lines. 0 = no extra space, 1 = one blank line (i.e. double-spaced from previous element).

ElementSyntax.description: Content-level syntax rules for a screenplay element.

required_prefix.description: A mandatory prefix string. Scene headings must start with 'INT.', 'EXT.', or 'INT./EXT.'.

prefix_options.description: If the prefix is one-of-many (e.g. scene headings can be INT., EXT., INT./EXT., I/E.), list all valid options here.

required_suffix.description: A mandatory suffix. Transitions typically end with a colon, e.g. 'CUT TO:'.

content_pattern.description: A regex pattern the element's content must match. Scene headings: '^(INT\.|EXT\.|INT\./EXT\.|I/E\.)\s+.+\s*-\s*(DAY|NIGHT|DAWN|DUSK|CONTINUOUS|LATER|SAME TIME|MOMENTS LATER)$'.

tense.description: Required grammatical tense. Action lines MUST be written in present tense.

structural_pattern.description: Human-readable structural description. For scene headings: 'INT./EXT. <LOCATION> - <TIME OF DAY>'. Three components separated by specific delimiters.

permitted_time_of_day_values.description: Recognized time-of-day designations for scene headings.

ContinuationRules.description: Rules for handling page breaks within or between elements.

dialogue_more_tag.description: Tag placed at the bottom of a page when dialogue is split across pages.

dialogue_continued_tag.description: Tag placed after the character name at the top of the next page when dialogue continues.

scene_continued_tag.description: Tag placed at the bottom/top of pages when a scene spans a page break. Used in shooting scripts; generally omitted in spec scripts.

use_scene_continued.description: Whether to use CONTINUED: tags at page breaks within scenes. Typically false for spec scripts, true for shooting scripts.

character_contd_for_interruption.description: Whether to append (CONT'D) to a character name when the same character speaks again after an action line interruption (non-page-break continuation).

page_number_position.description: Where page numbers appear. Hollywood standard is top-right.

first_page_numbered.description: Whether page 1 (the first page of content, after the title page) displays a page number. Hollywood convention: page 1 is unnumbered; numbering starts on page 2.

number_format.description: Format template for page numbers. '{n}.' produces '2.', '3.', etc.

header_text.description: Optional running header text (e.g. draft title). When absent, no header is rendered beyond the page number.

footer_text.description: Optional running footer text. When absent, no footer is rendered.

SceneNumberingRules.description: Scene numbering conventions. Present in shooting scripts, absent in spec scripts.

enabled.description: Whether scene numbers are displayed. False for spec scripts.

position.description: Where scene numbers appear relative to the scene heading.

format.description: Number format template. Some productions use letter suffixes for added scenes: '23A'.

added_scene_suffix.description: Characters used as suffixes when scenes are added between existing numbered scenes.

omitted_scene_notation.description: Text used when a previously numbered scene is removed but its number must be preserved for continuity.

TitlePageLayout.description: Layout and content rules for the screenplay title page.

fields.description: Ordered list of fields that appear on the title page.

counts_as_page.description: Whether the title page is counted in pagination. Hollywood standard: the title page is NOT counted; page 1 begins on the first page of content.

TitlePageField.description: A single field on the title page (title, author, contact info, etc.).

field_type.description: Discriminator for the type of title page field.

vertical_position.description: Vertical zone on the title page where this field appears.

horizontal_alignment.description: Horizontal alignment within the field's zone.

required_in_spec.description: Whether this field is required for a spec script submission.

required_in_shooting.description: Whether this field is required for a shooting script.

RevisionTracking.description: Revision color and marking conventions used in production scripts.

color_sequence.description: Ordered sequence of revision page colors. Industry standard is: White, Blue, Pink, Yellow, Green, Goldenrod, Buff, Salmon, Cherry, Tan, then second round prefixed with '2nd'.

revision_mark.description: Character placed in the right margin to indicate a revised line.

revision_date_in_header.description: Whether the revision date appears in the page header.

RevisionColor.description: A single entry in the revision color sequence.

order.description: Sequence position (1 = first revision, 2 = second, etc.).

color_name.description: Name of the paper color, e.g. 'Blue', 'Pink', 'Goldenrod'.

hex_code.description: Approximate hex color code for digital representation.

Duration.description: A time duration with explicit unit.

Extensions.description: Open extension object. Use namespaced keys (e.g. 'finaldraft.template_id', 'highland.fountain_compat') to avoid collisions with future schema properties.

notes.description: Required for custom variants: must explain the deviation from a named standard.
